title: Why Does Rejection Hurt Like a Physical Wound?
published_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime, set on actual publish
updated_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime
summary: In 2003, psychologist Naomi Eisenberger, with Matthew Lieberman and Kipling Williams, used fMRI and the Cyberball exclusion game to show that social rejection activates the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex — the same brain region tied to the distress of physical pain.
answer: TODO -- fill in before publish -- one paragraph direct answer; can adapt from script.md's cold open / closing reframe
duration_iso: TODO -- fill in before publish -- e.g. PT8M30S, fill in once the final render duration is known
key_findings: This wasn't actually the first hint that social separation and physical pain might share circuitry — it was just the first time anyone could see it directly in a living human brain.
There's a theory for why evolution would build it this way, proposed by researcher Jaak Panksepp: rather than building an entirely separate alarm system for social loss, evolution took a shortcut.

And here's the detail that turns this from an interesting brain-scan finding into something almost unsettling: if the systems really do overlap, a drug built to dull physical pain should, at least in theory, dull social pain too.
An over-the-counter painkiller, taken for something with no wound, no injury, no physical cause at all — and it measurably worked anyway.

sources.title: MacDonald, G. & Leary, M.R. (2005). Why does social exclusion hurt? The relationship between social and physical pain. Psychological Bulletin, 131(2), 202-223.
sources.publisher: Cross-linguistic use of physical-pain words for social pain; theoretical case that social exclusion is processed by the physical pain system

sources.title: Eisenberger, N.I., Lieberman, M.D. & Williams, K.D. (2003). Does rejection hurt? An fMRI study of social exclusion. Science, 302(5643), 290-292.
sources.publisher: Cyberball fMRI study -- dACC activation during exclusion correlated with distress; RVPFC activation correlated with regulation of that distress

sources.title: Panksepp, J., Vilberg, T., Bean, N.J., Coy, D.H. & Kastin, A.J. (1978). Reduction of distress vocalization in chicks by opiate-like peptides. Brain Research Bulletin, 3(6), 663-667.
sources.publisher: Distress vocalizations in isolated bird chicks reduced by opiate-like peptides -- early evidence of shared opioid circuitry between separation distress and physical pain

sources.title: MacLean, P.D. & Newman, J.D. (1988). Role of midline frontolimbic cortex in production of the isolation call of squirrel monkeys. Brain Research, 450(1-2), 111-123.
sources.publisher: Cingulate cortex lesions reducing separation/isolation distress calls in squirrel monkeys -- cross-species evidence for the same brain region governing separation response

sources.title: Panksepp, J. (2003). Neuroscience: Feeling the pain of social loss. Science, 302(5643), 237-239.
sources.publisher: Theoretical framing: the social-attachment system evolutionarily 'borrowed' the physical pain system's computations rather than evolving a separate alarm system

sources.title: DeWall, C.N., MacDonald, G., Webster, G.D., Masten, C.L., Baumeister, R.F., Powell, C., Combs, D., Schurtz, D.R., Stillman, T.F., Tice, D.M. & Eisenberger, N.I. (2010). Acetaminophen reduces social pain: Behavioral and neural evidence. Psychological Science, 21(7), 931-937.
sources.publisher: Acetaminophen (1,000mg/day, 3 weeks) reduced self-reported social pain (Exp. 1) and dACC/anterior insula neural response to Cyberball exclusion (Exp. 2)

sources.title: Somerville, L.H., Heatherton, T.F. & Kelley, W.M. (2006). Anterior cingulate cortex responds differentially to expectancy violation and social rejection. Nature Neuroscience, 9(8), 1007-1008; corroborated by Bolling, D.Z., Pitskel, N.B., Deen, B., Crowley, M.J., McPartland, J.C., Mayes, L.C. & Pelphrey, K.A. (2011). Dissociable brain mechanisms for processing social exclusion and rule violation. NeuroImage, 54(3), 2462-2471.
sources.publisher: Complication: dACC activation during Cyberball exclusion may partly reflect general expectancy-violation/surprise rather than social pain specifically -- a dissociable, nearby region tracks rule-violation separately from social rejection

sources.title: Hofman, S., Wieser, M.J. & van der Veen, F.M. (2021). Acetaminophen does not affect cardiac and brain responses to social rejection but seems to attenuate behavioral adaptation in a social judgment task. Social Neuroscience, 16(4), 362-374.
sources.publisher: Complication: a single-dose acetaminophen trial did not reproduce DeWall's neural/cardiac effects -- narrows the original finding to sustained dosing rather than contradicting it
